tweets.full_text:
『Claude Codeはプロンプト集ではありません』

この投稿はかなり整理されています。

Claude Codeのskillを、便利な小技ではなく「構造化された知識」として説明している点が重要です。

skillは、単なる指示文の寄せ集めではありません。

`SKILL.md` を中核にして、必要なら scripts、references、assets まで持てる。
つまり、Claudeにタスクのやり方を覚えさせるための、再利用可能な作業単位です。

特に大事なのは、段階的開示の考え方です。

常に読むのは YAML の要約だけ。
必要になった時だけ `SKILL.md` 本文を読む。
さらに必要な時だけ深い reference を掘る。

この構造があるから、毎回すべてを長文プロンプトで流し込まなくていい。トークンを抑えながら、専門性だけを必要な場面で呼び出せます。

もう一つ重要なのが、MCPとの役割分担です。

MCPは道具。
スキルはレシピ。

この切り分けができると、外部ツールをつないだだけで終わらず、どういう順番で何を判断するかまで含めて自動化できます。

たとえば、ファイルを読む、Slackに送る、Notionを更新する、といった操作はツールでできます。でも、いつ何を読み、どの順で検証し、どこで止まるかは別です。そこを毎回人間が口頭で補うのではなく、手順として閉じ込めるのがskillです。

だから、Claude Codeで再現性が出ない時に見るべきなのは、モデルの賢さより運用設計です。

何をトリガーにするのか。
どこまでをskillに閉じ込めるのか。
どの情報を常時ロードし、どこから先を遅延読込にするのか。

この設計が詰まると、出力の安定性が一段上がります。

Claudeを強くするのは、長いプロンプトではありません。

良いskill設計です。

Claude Codeを触っている人ほど、この整理は一度読む価値があります。

#ClaudeCode

tweets.full_text:
I discovered a Claude Code skill that does the entire job of an SEO agency.

It's called Toprank. Here's what happened when I ran it.

I typed /seo-analysis inside my repo. Claude asked for Search Console access, connected in one gcloud command, and started pulling data.

40 seconds later:

"Your homepage exists at two URLs. Google is splitting your ranking authority between them. Here's the fix."

"You have two pages targeting 'pricing for X.' Neither is winning. Here's how to consolidate."

"This page gets 600 impressions a month and ranks 47th. The title tag doesn't match the search query people are actually using. Here's the rewrite."

I typed "implement everything."

Three minutes later, every fix was staged and ready to push.

The /content-writer skill runs automatically when it finds content gaps and writes full blog posts with proper structure, E-E-A-T signals, and internal linking plans.

This is what $3,000/month used to buy.

MIT License. 100% Opensource. https://t.co/I8o6CAUSvd

user.name: Ihtesham Ali
user.description: investor, writer, educator, and a dragon ball fan 🐉

tweets.full_text:
Most companies are treating AI tools like personal calculators. The teams that build a shared "Team OS" are playing a completely different game.

Here's why. When one engineer writes a Claude Code skill, they save themselves maybe 20 minutes per use. When that skill lives in a shared GitHub repo, every engineer on the team saves 20 minutes per use. A 10-person team running 5 shared skills daily is recovering 16+ hours of engineering time per week. That number only goes up as skills improve.

The compounding part is what gets interesting. Every skill your team builds becomes training data for the next skill. Your deploy automation informs your PR review skill. Your PR review skill informs your testing skill. Six months in, your Team OS has absorbed hundreds of micro-decisions that used to live in one person's head.

This is the same dynamic that made internal wikis valuable in the 2010s, except the knowledge actually executes instead of sitting in a Confluence page nobody reads.

The companies that figure this out first are going to open a gap that's nearly impossible to close. Because you can't just copy someone's Team OS. The skills encode your codebase patterns, your deployment quirks, your team's actual workflow. It's institutional memory that runs on autopilot.

The open source ecosystem already has 200+ skill repos and frameworks for syncing skills across tools. The infrastructure exists. The question is whether your team starts building the flywheel now or tries to catch up in 12 months when the gap is 10x wider.

tweets.full_text: Claude Code Spring 2026 Update Roundup — Mastering auto mode, /ultrareview, and xhigh effort https://t.co/XbZ3ykVNX3 https://t.co/NhYJt8qpum

I built an auto-proposal generator in 6 hours with Claude Code that turns discovery call notes into custom proposals in under 2 minutes. Here's the build: 1. Asked Claude to write a script that takes structured input and outputs a formatted PDF proposal
2. It used Python with ReportLab library for PDF generation
3. Created a template with placeholders for client name, pain points, deliverables, pricing, and timeline
4. Built a simple web form using Flask where I paste my call notes after every discovery call
5. Form fields: client name, industry, main problem, current revenue, goals, budget range
6. Script parses the input and populates the proposal template dynamically
7. Added conditional logic so pricing tiers adjust based on their stated budget
8. Generates a branded PDF and saves it to a Dropbox folder automatically
9. Connected it to Make so when the PDF hits Dropbox it triggers an email via Gmail
10. Email goes to the prospect with the proposal attached and a calendar link to discuss
11. Mistake 1: forgot to validate email addresses, sent a few proposals to broken emails
12. Fixed by adding regex validation on the form submission
13. Mistake 2: PDFs looked generic, no client-specific personalization beyond the basics
14. Added a section that pulls recent news about their business using a Google search API
15. Total cost: $0 for the script, $3/month for a basic Flask hosting on Render Went from spending 90 minutes per proposal to under 2 minutes. AI won't replace you but someone using AI to move faster will. sharing what I'm building with Claude Code + Make daily. follow if you want to see the builds.

animation isn't a separate step anymore. 

canvas is code, graphics are code → just ask @claudeai to animate next, same canvas.

it's just another thing you design *together* with claude.

this is me animating a chart on @lunagraphHQ:
> click any element
> ⌘+L to reference it (0:10) 
> ask claude to animate
> see it live on the same canvas

this one's a candlestick chart, needs to behave like real TradingView, with open/close logic, follow the legend, etc.

2-3 prompts in, probably 1-2 more to nail it.

Automating the post-training grind like this could massively accelerate open ML development. 

The demo of it pushing GPQA accuracy from ~10% to 39% in 10 hours (beating Claude Code) is seriously impressive.

Can't wait to try the CLI and Space. The future of shipping better models just got a lot more accessible.
